title: Get to Know 211info: Staff Stories #2 - 211info
text: Who is 211info? We’re so much more than a contact center. We’re a dedicated and dynamic team from all walks of life who live and work within the communities we serve. We have come together over shared values and a passion for helping others. Over the past few years, our agency capabilities have also expanded to include more dynamic partnerships in emergency incident response, specialized resource navigation, health care, care coordination, and statewide outreach. Each day, 211info staff show up with kindness, listen with care, and ask the right questions to uncover every possible option to help people find the resources they need. That human connection is the heart of our agency, and the true value of using 211 services. Read on to learn more about three of our staff members. Bridget, Community Information Specialist 211info tenure: 11 years Location: Multnomah County Why is 211info (or 211 as a service) important to you? Why do you work at 211info ? It’s a stable, caring, and comfortable environment. I enjoy helping people, but as an introvert, I appreciate being able to work with the community over the phone instead of in person. I also used these services as a young mom many years ago. What about your position at 211info makes you smile or feel good about the work you do? I can empathize with what callers are going through, so when I am able to offer someone who is struggling support, it makes a difference. Even if there are no resources available, people are often so grateful to just have someone there who is able to listen and share some kind words. What’s something you wish that more people in the community knew about 211 as a service, or about 211info as an organization? That some of our staff have been exactly where they are, and we know how it feels. Also that some of us utilize 211 services to find help when we need it too. What’s the biggest misconception about 211 services that you notice in your job or personal life? That we offer direct services. We only connect people to the assistance by helping identify what’s available and how to access it. How would you describe your job to a 6-year-old? We try to help people who are in a tough situation get what they need. What’s your favorite food? Calamari! What’s something you’re most proud of over the past year? That I’ve been more mindful and intentional about my health. And that I’m still a 211info employee. What is the best piece of advice you’ve ever received? Try to leave the world a better place than how you found it. When do you feel the most courageous? When I’m doing what I need to do for me in order to take good care of myself. Gabby, SNAP Access Coordinator 211info tenure: Almost five years Location: Washington County Why is 211info (or 211 as a service) important to you? Why do you work at 211info ? I worked in call centers for 12 years before coming to 211info, and I’m thankful for the opportunity to take that experience and turn it into making a real difference in people’s lives. I love being a helper and working in a place that is so welcoming and caring. The human connection we offer means a lot to me. When I talk to people in the community, they often say, “Oh, I know 211! That’s how you get help.” I feel lucky to be part of something like that. Are there any memorable moments you reflect back on that make you smile or feel good about the work you do at 211info? At my first outreach event, we helped about 25 farm families complete rental assistance applications. What’s something you wish more people in the community knew about 211 as a service, or about 211info as an organization? That contacting 211 for help is safe and anonymous. We don’t collect personal details, and we don’t share identifying information with anyone, including government agencies. We may ask general demographic questions, like age, zip code, income, or pregnancy status, only so we can match people with the best resources. Sharing that information is always optional and doesn’t change the level of service someone receives. What’s the biggest misconception about 211 services that you notice in your job or personal life? People incorrectly assume that 211 provides the actual financial assistance or that we choose who gets the assistance. How would you describe your job to a 6-year-old? If your mom needs help finding food, she can call me, and I’ll help her. What’s your favorite food? I like to try a bit of everything, but sweets are always my favorite. What’s something you’re most proud of recently? My family! My kids finished school and got a better job, my husband was promoted, and my son got married. What is the best piece of advice you’ve ever received? Choose your battles. Kelly, Resource Specialist 211info tenure: Five and a half years Location: Clark County Why is 211info (or 211 as a service) important to you? Why do you work at 211info? Working at 211info gives me the chance to be a helper. I enjoy guiding people to the services they need, and helping make complex systems easier to understand while easing some of their stress along the way. Like during severe weather, connecting unhoused individuals with safe shelter is just one part of what we do. 211 is an important community service that reflects the compassion of our staff to the public. The most rewarding moments are when a caller tells me I’ve given them hope, that’s when I know this work truly matters, and reminds me I’m exactly where I need to be. Are there any special moments you look back on that always make you feel good about the work you do? During my first winter at 211info, I received a call on a Saturday morning about an elderly person living in their car. They had lost both their partner and only son, and had been priced out of their home. I provided multiple referrals and told the caller I hoped one of the resources would help get them out of the cold. I’ve thought about this person many times over the past five years. Their story did have a happy ending, as local media heard about it, a GoFundMe was created, and the person spent the rest of their life safely housed. What’s something you wish more people in the community knew about 211 as a service, or about 211info as an organization? I wish people could see 211info from the inside, because they’d witness the compassion of the employees and the lengths they go to help others. What’s the biggest misconception about 211 services that you notice in your job or personal life? That we are the same or an extension of 311. Although we’re partners, many 211s are nationally accredited Information and Referral (I&R) agencies that adhere to rigorous database and resource standardization and update requirements. How would you describe your job to a 6-year-old? I help people find out who to go to if they need help with food, clothing, paying bills, and other big people things. What’s your favorite food? Thai food, fresh veggies, and fruit! What’s something you’re most proud of over the past year? I’m proud of my volunteer work with my neighborhood association and the Feral Cat Coalition of Oregon. What is the best piece of advice you’ve ever received? My dad always said, “You don’t know if you don’t ask.” That also extended to his way of teaching that, “You don’t know if you can do it if you don’t try.” When do you feel the most courageous? I feel most courageous when I know people in need or in danger need my help. This post is part of a series where we introduce our board of directors and a wide variety of 211info staff. Check out past editions in the series with Staff Stories #1 , and meet board members Erick Maddox and Piseth Pich .
